content:
# Leviticus Chapter 11 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 63v–64r | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus pp. 95–96

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses and Aaron: **2** "Speak to the people of Israel and say: These are the living creatures you may eat from among all the animals on the land.

**3** Any animal that has split hooves — hooves divided in two — and that chews its cud: you may eat it. **4** But you must not eat the following animals that only chew their cud or only have split hooves:

The camel — it chews the cud but does not have split hooves; it is unclean for you. **5** The rock badger — it chews the cud but does not have split hooves; it is unclean for you. **6** The hare — it chews the cud but does not have split hooves; it is unclean for you. **7** The pig — it does have split hooves, hooves divided in two, but it does not chew the cud; it is unclean for you. **8** You must not eat their flesh, and you must not touch their carcasses; they are unclean for you.

**9** "Of all the creatures living in the water — in the seas or the rivers — you may eat any creature that has fins and scales. **10** But every creature in the seas or rivers that does not have fins and scales — among all the swarming things of the water, among all the living things in the water — they are detestable to you. **11** They shall remain detestable to you; you must not eat their flesh, and you must regard their carcasses as detestable. **12** Every creature in the water that does not have fins and scales is detestable to you.

**13** "Of birds, these are the ones you are to regard as detestable — they must not be eaten; they are detestable:

the eagle, the vulture, the black vulture, **14** the red kite, black kites of every kind, **15** every kind of raven, **16** the horned owl, the screech owl, the seagull, hawks of every kind, **17** the little owl, the cormorant, the great owl, **18** the white owl, the desert owl, the osprey, **19** the stork, herons of every kind, the hoopoe, and the bat.

**20** "All flying insects that walk on all fours are detestable to you. **21** But these flying insects that walk on all fours you may eat — those that have jointed legs above their feet for leaping on the ground. **22** Of these you may eat: the locust of every kind, the bald locust of every kind, the cricket of every kind, and the grasshopper of every kind. **23** But all other flying insects that have four legs are detestable to you.

**24** "By the following creatures you will become unclean. Anyone who touches their carcass will be unclean until evening, **25** and anyone who picks up their carcass must wash their clothes — and they will be unclean until evening.

**26** "Every animal that has hooves but is not fully split and does not chew the cud is unclean for you; anyone who touches it becomes unclean. **27** Every animal that walks on its paws — of all the animals that walk on all fours — is unclean for you. Anyone who touches their carcass will be unclean until evening, **28** and the one who picks up their carcass must wash their clothes and will be unclean until evening. These are unclean for you.

**29** "Of the animals that swarm on the ground, these are unclean for you: the mole rat, the mouse, the great lizard of every kind, **30** the gecko, the monitor lizard, the common lizard, the skink, and the chameleon. **31** Among all the swarming creatures, these are unclean for you. Anyone who touches them when they are dead will be unclean until evening.

**32** "When any of them dies and falls on something, that object will be unclean — whether it is a wooden article, a piece of clothing, a leather item, a sack, or any other utensil used for work. It must be put in water and will remain unclean until evening; then it will be clean. **33** If any of them falls into a clay pot, everything in it becomes unclean and you must break the pot. **34** Any food that could be eaten, on which such water comes, becomes unclean; and any drinkable liquid in any such vessel becomes unclean. **35** Anything that one of their carcasses falls on becomes unclean: an oven or a cooking stand must be broken down — they are unclean and shall remain unclean for you. **36** But a spring or a cistern holding water remains clean; anyone who touches one of their carcasses becomes unclean. **37** If one of their carcasses falls on seed grain that is to be planted, it remains clean. **38** But if water has been put on the seed and one of their carcasses falls on it, that seed is unclean for you.

**39** "If an animal that you are permitted to eat dies on its own, anyone who touches its carcass will be unclean until evening. **40** Anyone who eats from its carcass must wash their clothes and will be unclean until evening. Anyone who picks up its carcass must also wash their clothes and will be unclean until evening.

**41** "Every creature that swarms on the ground is detestable; it must not be eaten. **42** Whether it moves on its belly, or goes on all fours, or has many legs — any creature that swarms on the ground — you must not eat them, because they are detestable. **43** Do not make yourselves detestable with any swarming creature, and do not defile yourselves with them, so that you become unclean through them.

**44** For I am the LORD your God. Consecrate yourselves, therefore, and be holy — because I am holy. You must not defile yourselves with any of the swarming creatures that swarm on the ground. **45** For I am the LORD, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt to be your God. You shall be holy, because I am holy.

**46** "This is the instruction for animals and birds and every living creature that moves through the water and every creature that swarms on the ground, **47** to make a distinction between the unclean and the clean — between the animals that may be eaten and those that must not be eaten."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**The structure of the whole chapter — binary logic and embodied theology.** Leviticus 11 is organized around a single recurring question: does this creature belong to the category of *tahor* (clean) or *tame* (unclean)? These are not primarily hygienic or health-based categories. They are ontological categories — ways of marking the boundaries of the world into what fits and what does not fit within the life of the holy people. The structural pattern is consistently binary: two criteria must be satisfied simultaneously, not one. Land animals require both split hooves AND cud-chewing. Water creatures require both fins AND scales. The chapter is an exercise in double-criteria logic, and the animals that fail are precisely those who satisfy only one condition — the half-right animals, the creatures that seem to belong but don't quite make it. This is not accidental. Leviticus is built on the maintenance of boundaries, and the animals that violate the categories are those that blur boundaries. The chapter is less a health code than a curriculum in paying attention to distinctions.

**"You may eat" and "you must not eat" — the grammar of holiness.** The chapter never says that the unclean animals are harmful, dangerous, or morally inferior. It says they are *tame* (unclean) and *sheketz* (detestable). The word *sheketz* is related to a root meaning "to abominate" — it is the strongest term of ritual aversion in the vocabulary of Leviticus. Yet the rationale offered is not practical but theological: "I am the LORD your God" (v. 44) and "I am the LORD who brought you out of Egypt" (v. 45). The dietary laws are not grounded in Israelite experience of food-borne illness but in Israel's identity as a people set apart. What Israel eats every day at the table mirrors what Israel is before God: a people that maintains distinctions, that does not collapse all things into one undifferentiated mass, that pays attention to the lines the Creator has drawn.

**The four "problem" land animals — why those four?** The text lists exactly four animals that satisfy only one of the two criteria for clean land animals: the camel (chews cud, no split hoof), the rock badger or *shaphan* (chews cud or appears to, no split hoof), the hare or *arnevet* (chews cud or appears to, no split hoof), and the pig (split hoof, no cud-chewing). The selection seems deliberate — these are four animals common in the ancient Near East, recognizable to the Israelite audience, and each one a prominent example of the wrong-in-exactly-one-way type. Modern readers have noted that the rock badger and hare do not technically chew cud in the biological sense, but they do make a chewing motion with their mouths that ancient observers would have interpreted as cud-chewing. Leviticus is describing the phenomenal world as it appears, not the anatomical world as dissected. The pig's inclusion is culturally loaded: in the ancient Near East, pig sacrifice was associated with Canaanite and later Hellenistic religious practice. The prohibition on pork thus carried a double weight — dietary and identity-marking. To refuse pork is to refuse to belong to those people.

**The pig's asymmetric notoriety.** Of the four "failed" land animals, the pig receives the most attention in subsequent Jewish history — not because the text emphasizes it but because it became the site of intense cultural pressure. The Maccabean crisis (2nd century BCE) centered precisely on the forced eating of pork as a test of Hellenistic loyalty. Antiochus IV Epiphanes used pig sacrifice and forced pork consumption as instruments of cultural erasure; Jews died rather than eat it. By the time the New Testament is written, "pig" has become a shorthand for everything outside the covenant people (see Luke 15:15-16, the prodigal son feeding pigs, or Matthew 7:6). The book of Leviticus, in listing the pig as unclean among three less-notorious animals, could not have anticipated the weight it was handing to a future of persecution. The pig became the text's most consequential animal.

**Water creatures: the fins-and-scales requirement.** The clean/unclean distinction for water creatures is simpler than for land animals: fins and scales. Everything that has both is clean; everything else — shellfish, squid, eels, catfish, sharks, dolphins, and so on — is unclean. The word used for the unclean water creatures is *sheketz* ("detestable"), the same word used for the flying insects. They are not merely unclean; they are *abominable* in the ritual sense — the maximum term of aversion. The word appears four times in vv. 10–12 alone, hammering the point home with a rhetorical intensity that the translation must honor. I have rendered *sheketz* throughout as "detestable" — not "abomination" (which in contemporary English carries moral-sexual connotations it does not have in Hebrew) and not "abominable" (which is archaic). "Detestable" is both accurate and carries the visceral force of the original.

**The bird list — identification problems.** The bird list in verses 13-19 is notoriously difficult. The Hebrew names for many of these birds do not have certain English equivalents; ancient ornithology did not classify birds the way modern science does, and the identification of specific species from ancient Hebrew terms is genuinely uncertain. The list appears to follow an ecological logic — birds of prey and carrion-feeders dominate the list. The raven-group is broad (*kol-'orev le-mino*, "every raven according to its kind"). The hoopoe (*dukhifat*) is conspicuous among mostly predatory birds as a non-predator; it appears here because of its associations with unclean feeding habits in rabbinic tradition (it was thought to nest in dung). The bat (*atallef*) appears at the end — a flying creature with no feathers, neither quite a bird nor quite something else. Its placement confirms that the list is phenomenological, organized by observed behavior and habitat rather than by modern taxonomic categories. I have used modern English bird names where the identification is reasonably secure and preserved the uncertainty where it is not.

**Flying insects — the two-hoop requirement and the locusts.** The clean/unclean distinction for flying insects is also binary: wings plus four legs is normally disqualifying, BUT the insect must also have jointed leaping legs above the feet. This is the locust type — orthoptera: locusts, crickets, grasshoppers. The rationale appears ecological: locusts are airborne but also terrestrial; they leap rather than simply walk; they are clean. The rabbinic tradition would later spend considerable energy identifying exactly which of the 800+ species of orthoptera qualify as clean, producing lists of regional variants. John the Baptist ate locusts (Mark 1:6) — a detail that situates him firmly within the clean-food world of Leviticus 11, the prophet-in-the-desert living on what the Torah permits. The text's permission of locusts is not an eccentricity; it is the provision for people in wilderness conditions.

**Carcass contamination — degrees of severity and material contagion.** Verses 24-40 are the most technically detailed section of the chapter, dealing with what happens when one comes into contact with the carcass of an unclean animal — or even, in the case of the permitted animals, when a clean animal dies of itself (v. 39). The system has remarkable precision. Touching: unclean until evening. Carrying: unclean until evening, plus washing of clothes. Objects: contaminated if touched, must be soaked in water, then clean by evening. Clay pots: irreparably contaminated, must be broken. Ovens and cooking stands: broken down. Springs and cisterns: uncontaminated. Seed: uncontaminated unless wet. The system is not random; it maps a logic of porosity. Sealed vessels contaminate their contents entirely. Open containers can be washed. Stone and water are resilient. Clay is irreparably permeable. The chapter is, among other things, a theology of materials — some things can be cleansed of contamination; others cannot and must be destroyed.

**The swarming creatures — *sherets* and the primal chaos.** The word *sherets* (often translated "swarming things") appears throughout Leviticus 11 and echoes back to Genesis 1, where the same word describes the creatures that swarm in the water when God creates them (*yishretsu ha-mayim sherets nefesh chayah*, Gen 1:20). In Genesis, the swarming creatures are good — they fill the world at God's command. In Leviticus 11, the swarming creatures (ground-swarming specifically) are entirely prohibited. Something has shifted: the swarming creatures that teem on the ground are those most associated with dirt, decay, the decomposing boundary between life and death. They are not creation-good in the way that birds and fish and cattle are; they inhabit the margins where things break down. To eat them would be to take the margin into oneself. This is not about disgust psychology, though disgust is part of the phenomenology. It is about the creature that is in-between — neither cleanly of the sky nor cleanly of the earth, crawling on bellies (v. 42: "on its belly" = the serpent's posture, the creature cursed in Gen 3:14), moving through the membrane between life and death.

**"Be holy, for I am holy" — the theological climax.** Verse 44 is the theological axis on which the entire chapter — and much of Leviticus — turns. *Qedoshim tih'yu ki qadosh ani YHWH Eloheikhem* — "You shall be holy, because I am holy, I the LORD your God." The logic is participatory: Israel's holiness is not self-generated. It is a reflection of, a participation in, the holiness of the God who chose them. The word *qadosh* (holy, set-apart) shares a root with *qiddush* (sanctification), *miqdash* (sanctuary), and *kiddushin* (marriage-consecration). To be holy is to be set apart for a specific relationship, a specific purpose, a specific one. The dietary laws are not the cause of Israel's holiness; they are the daily practice of it. Every meal becomes an enacted reminder: we are a people who make distinctions, because our God is a God who makes distinctions — between light and darkness (Gen 1:4), between waters above and below (Gen 1:6), between Israel and the nations (Lev 20:26), and between the clean and the unclean (Lev 11:47). The table is a liturgy.

**Verse 45 — "who brought you up out of Egypt."** The placement of the Exodus reference in verse 45 is striking. In the middle of the dietary regulations, in the closing theological summary, the text grounds the holiness requirement not in creation but in redemption: "I am the LORD who brought you up out of the land of Egypt to be your God." The dietary laws are not natural law, not universal ethics — they are covenant markers for a particular people saved by a particular act. "Brought you up" (*ha-ma'aleh etkhem*, using *alah* — the same root as the burnt offering that goes up) — is unusual phrasing; the more common formula is "brought you out" (*yatsa*). The use of *alah* here may be deliberate: the Exodus is an ascent, a being lifted up. The people who were lifted out of slavery are now called to lift themselves into holiness — to match, in their daily practice at the table, the upward movement God made for them.

**The closing torah summary (vv. 46-47) — "instruction" and the two lists.** The chapter ends with a formal summary: *zot torat ha-behemah ve-ha-of* — "this is the instruction for the animal and the bird." The word *torah* here (SOPHIA ruling: "instruction") is the book's own self-description. This chapter is not a code, not a law in the Roman legal sense, but an *instruction* — a teaching, a showing of the way. The two verbs of the final verse — *le-havdil* (to divide, to distinguish) and *le-havdil bein* (between) — echo the cosmic separations of Genesis 1. The Creator who divided light from darkness, water from water, land from sea now calls Israel to enact those same divisions at the table: between the unclean and the clean, between the eatable and the uneatable. The dietary laws are creation-theology enacted with the mouth.

**LXX (Vaticanus) observations — pages 95-96.** Codex Vaticanus pages 95 and 96 (Vat.gr.1209 images /0099 and /0100) carry Leviticus 11 in the characteristic three-column uncial format. Page 95 begins with the chapter marker visible in the margin (LXX chapter enumeration differs from the MT; Leviticus 11 in the LXX is approximately chapters 37-38 in the older LXX chapter count, though the Vaticanus chapter markers in this section reflect a transitional system).

At verse 3, the LXX renders *mafrisas parsa* ("split hoof") as *διχηλοῦν ὁπλήν* — "dividing the hoof/splitting the hoof." The verb *dichēloō* (*dicha* = in two, *hēlos* = hoof-nail) is a precise technical term; Vaticanus preserves it throughout the animal list with consistency, showing careful attention to the zoological precision of the Hebrew.

At verse 4, the camel (*gamal*) is rendered ΚΑΜΗΛΟΝ (*kamēlon*) — a clear loanword from Semitic into Greek, confirming that the camel was well-known in the Hellenistic world but retained its foreign name. The rock badger (*shaphan*) becomes ΧΟΙΡΟΓΡΎΛΛΙΟΝ (*choirogyllion* — literally "pig-squeaker" or "pig-mouse"), a Greek term that attempts to describe the animal by its sounds and appearance. The hare (*arnevet*) becomes ΔΑΣΎΠΟΥΣ (*dasypoūs* — "hairy/rough foot"), a Greek descriptive term rather than a name. These coinages reveal the LXX translators working with animals they knew but for which Greek had no settled vocabulary.

At verses 10-12, the LXX translates *sheketz* (detestable) as ΒΔΈΛΥΓΜΑ (*bdelygma* — abomination, something that causes revulsion). This Greek word appears in the New Testament in the phrase *bdelygma tēs erēmōseōs* (Matthew 24:15; Mark 13:14) — "the abomination of desolation," a phrase drawn from Daniel but filtered through the LXX vocabulary of Leviticus-style ritual aversion. The dietary abomination (*bdelygma*) and the eschatological abomination (*bdelygma*) share a word — which, in the LXX world, means they share a conceptual register.

At verse 44, the LXX renders *qedoshim tih'yu* as *ἅγιοι ἔσεσθε* (*hagioi esesthe* — "you shall be holy") — precisely the form quoted verbatim in 1 Peter 1:16, where the author cites Leviticus 11:44 from the LXX: *διότι γέγραπται· ἅγιοι ἔσεσθε, ὅτι ἐγὼ ἅγιός* ("because it is written: you shall be holy, because I am holy"). The quotation proves that the author of 1 Peter was reading Vaticanus-type text. More importantly: 1 Peter applies a dietary-law holiness command to Gentile Christians who do not observe the dietary laws. The trajectory from Leviticus 11:44 to 1 Peter 1:16 is the trajectory of the entire New Testament's reading of Leviticus — the dietary laws as vehicles for a holiness-command that transcends the particular practices, the *sheketz* animals pointing toward a deeper set-apartness that the New Testament community is called to embody in different form.

---

*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 63v–64r, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 95–96 (LXX).*
